  • Download Rmd

Branch-specific rate estimation (R2.2) and error vs trend (R2.3)

Supplementary analyses addressing Reviewer 2, comments R2.2 and R2.3.

  • R2.2: Branch-specific rate estimation errors from the OBDD calibration study.
  • R2.3: Estimation error as a function of the trend parameter.

Uses only the existing MCMC parameter traces from the calibration study (no re-simulation or posterior tree files needed).

Load calibration data

Number of datasets: 500

R2.2: Branch-specific rate estimation

In the OBDD model, branch-specific rates evolve according to a geometric Brownian motion parameterised by the root rates (\(\lambda_0\), \(\mu_0\)), trend parameters (\(\alpha_\lambda\), \(\alpha_\mu\)), and diffusion parameters (\(\sigma_\lambda\), \(\sigma_\mu\)). These six parameters fully determine the distribution of branch-specific rates across the tree. If they are accurately recovered, the implied branch-rate distribution is accurately recovered.

Recovery of GBM parameters

Root rate recovery

Implied rate heterogeneity

Under the GBM, the variance of log-rates across branches at time \(t\) from the root is \(\sigma^2 t\). For a tree of height \(h\), the expected standard deviation of log-\(\lambda\) across lineages at the tips is approximately \(\sigma_\lambda \sqrt{h}\). We can compare the implied heterogeneity from the true vs. posterior \(\sigma\) estimates.

NMSE of GBM parameters vs tree size

R2.3: Estimation error vs trend parameter

The reviewer predicts that in models with a positive net-diversification trend (\(\alpha_\lambda > 0\) or \(\alpha_\mu < 0\)), the estimation error should be smaller because the tree is expected to be larger and more informative.

NMSE vs speciation trend \(\alpha_\lambda\)

NMSE vs net diversification trend \(\alpha_\lambda - \alpha_\mu\)

NMSE vs extinction trend \(\alpha_\mu\)

Tree size as confound

The reviewer’s hypothesis is that positive net-diversification trends produce larger trees, which provide more information. We verify this expected confound.
